## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Biochemical Basis of Fermentation

The underlying chemistry of all fermented milk products can be summarized in three logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Substrate Hydrolysis:** The LAB secrete the enzyme **β-galactosidase (lactase)**, which hydrolyses milk **lactose (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁)** into its constituent monosaccharides, **glucose and galactose**.
- **Step 2 — Glycolysis (Embden–Meyerhof Pathway):** Glucose is catabolized via EMP pathway to **pyruvic acid (CH₃COCOOH)**.
- **Step 3 — Lactic Acid Fermentation:** Pyruvate is reduced by the enzyme **lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)** in the presence of NADH to form **lactic acid (CH₃CHOHCOOH)**. The reaction regenerates NAD⁺, sustaining glycolysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" matters:** The accumulation of lactic acid causes a sharp drop in pH (typically from **6.6 → 4.4**). At this pH, **casein micelles** lose their stabilizing k-casein layer (the "hairy layer") and aggregate, forming a continuous protein network — this is the **acid-induced gel** responsible for the semi-solid texture of yogurt, dahi, and similar products.

### 2.2 Classification of Fermented Milk

| Class | Optimum Temp | Key Microbes | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mesophilic | **20 – 30 °C** | *Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis*, *Lc. cremoris*, *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* | Cultured buttermilk, Kefir (mixed), Lassi |
| Thermophilic | **40 – 45 °C** | *Streptococcus thermophilus*, *Lactobacillus delbrueckii* subsp. *bulgaricus* | Yogurt, Dahi, Bulgarian milk, Acidophilus milk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbiotic Pair (Thermophilic):** *S. thermophilus* grows first, producing formic acid that stimulates *Lb. bulgaricus*; in turn, *Lb. bulgaricus* liberates peptides (glycine, histidine) that accelerate *S. thermophilus* growth. This **proto-cooperation** is a classic KTU exam point.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Lactose → Lactic Acid (stoichiometry) | $C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} + H_2O \rightarrow 4\,CH_3CHOHCOOH$ | 1 mol lactose → 4 mol lactic acid |
| Theoretical yield of lactic acid from lactose | $Y = \dfrac{4 \times 90}{342} = 1.053$ | g lactic acid per g lactose |
| Titratable Acidity (TA) | $\%\,LA = \dfrac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times 9 \times 100}{V_{sample} \times W_{sample}}$ | 9 = equivalent weight of lactic acid ÷ 10 |
| Developed Acidity (DA) | $DA = TA_{final} - TA_{initial}$ | Expressed in **% lactic acid** |
| pH–TA empirical relationship | $pH \approx 6.20 - 1.50 \times TA$ | Valid for TA in 0.3 – 1.2 % LA |
| Cooling load (rate of heat removal) | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ; m = mass of curd, c_p ≈ **3.93 kJ/(kg·K)** for milk/curd |
| Casein isoelectric point | $pI_{casein} \approx 4.6$ | Coagulation begins ≈ pH **5.2**, complete by **4.6** |
| Incubation time (thermophilic) | $t = \dfrac{\ln(N_f/N_0)}{\mu_{max}} \cdot \ln 2$ | $\mu_{max} \approx 0.6\,h^{-1}$ for yogurt culture |
| Standardized MSNF | $MSNF = \dfrac{SNF \times 100}{100 - fat}$ | % (w/w) |
| Formaldehyde titration (protein) | $\%\,Protein = \dfrac{1.7 \times 5 \times 0.1 \times 100}{V}$ | For 5 mL sample, factor 1.7 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline of note:** $\%\,LA = \dfrac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times 9 \times 100}{V_{sample} \times W_{sample}}$. The constant **9** arises because the equivalent weight of lactic acid is **90**, divided by **10** (since 10 mL sample is typically titrated in milk analysis).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Design:** Stoichiometry guides substrate dosing and predicts titratable acidity endpoints in industrial yogurt vats.
- **Quality Control:** TA measurement (Soxhlet–Henkel degree) is a **mandatory QC release parameter** in dairy plants.
- **Shelf-life Modeling:** pH and TA govern microbial stability and are inputs to predictive shelf-life models (e.g., Arrhenius-based).
- **Sensory Science:** TA, acetaldehyde, and diacetyl concentrations together predict consumer perception of "tanginess."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometry of Lactose Conversion to Lactic Acid

We need to express the quantitative yield of lactic acid generated from a given mass of lactose.

**Step 1 — Write the balanced reaction.**

$$C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} + H_2O \rightarrow 4\,CH_3CHOHCOOH$$

**Step 2 — Tabulate molecular weights.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{lactose} &= (12 \times 12) + (22 \times 1) + (11 \times 16) \\
&= 144 + 22 + 176 \\
&= 342\;g/mol \\
M_{lactic\,acid} &= (3 \times 12) + (6 \times 1) + (3 \times 16) \\
&= 36 + 6 + 48 \\
&= 90\;g/mol
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply mole-to-mass ratio.**

Since 1 mol lactose yields 4 mol lactic acid:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of LA from 1 mol lactose} &= 4 \times 90 = 360\;g \\
\text{Mass of lactose} &= 342\;g \\
\text{Yield factor} &= \frac{360}{342} = 1.0526 \approx 1.053\;g\,LA/g\,lactose
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Generalize for an arbitrary lactose mass $m_L$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{LA,\,theoretical} &= 1.053 \times m_L \\
\text{Industrial yield } \eta &\approx 0.85 - 0.95 \\
\therefore m_{LA,\,actual} &= \eta \times 1.053 \times m_L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic Row:** The factor 1.053 is central to predicting how much acid a milk of known lactose content (typically **4.6 – 4.9 % w/w** in cow milk) can produce, which sets the **theoretical maximum TA** at full fermentation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Titratable Acidity from Standard NaOH Titration

**Given:** $V_{NaOH}$ mL of $N_{NaOH}$ NaOH is required to neutralize the acid in $V_{sample}$ mL of fermented milk (density $\rho \approx 1.030\,g/mL$).

**Step 1 — Equivalents of base used.**

$$
\text{Equivalents}_{NaOH} = V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH}
$$

**Step 2 — Equivalents of lactic acid (1:1 neutralization).**

$$
\text{Equivalents}_{LA} = \text{Equivalents}_{NaOH}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of lactic acid in the sample.**

$$
m_{LA} = \text{Equivalents}_{LA} \times E_{LA}
$$

where $E_{LA} = 90$ (equivalent weight of lactic acid, since n-factor = 1).

**Step 4 — Express as % w/w of the sample.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,LA &= \frac{m_{LA}}{m_{sample}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times 90}{V_{sample} \times \rho} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Standardize for $V_{sample} = 10$ mL and $\rho = 1.030$ g/mL.**

$$
\%\,LA = \frac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times 9 \times 100}{10}
$$

This is the canonical **% Lactic Acid** formula used in dairy labs.

**Conversion Logic Row:** The 9 in the formula combines $\dfrac{90 \times \rho \times 100}{V_{sample}}$, reducing to 9 for the standard 10 mL sample and ρ ≈ 1.030.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Process Parameters

| Parameter | Yogurt (Thermophilic) | Cultured Buttermilk (Mesophilic) | Kefir (Mixed) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fermentation Temp | **40 – 45 °C** | **20 – 30 °C** | **20 – 25 °C** |
| Fermentation Time | **4 – 6 h** | **14 – 16 h** | **18 – 24 h** |
| Target pH (end) | **4.4 – 4.6** | **4.4 – 4.6** | **4.4 – 4.5** |
| Target TA | **0.85 – 0.95 % LA** | **0.80 – 0.90 % LA** | **0.75 – 0.90 % LA** |
| Symbiotic Pair | *S. thermophilus* + *Lb. bulgaricus* | *Lc. lactis* + *Lc. cremoris* + *Leuc. mesenteroides* | Kefir grains (yeasts + LAB + acetic bacteria) |
| Key Aroma | **Acetaldehyde** (green-apple) | **Diacetyl** (buttery) | **Ethanol + CO₂** (slight fizz) |
| Body | Firm gel | Viscous, pourable | Slightly effervescent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The **aroma compound** question is a high-frequency 2-mark question. **Yogurt → acetaldehyde**, **Buttermilk → diacetyl**, **Kefir → ethanol + CO₂**, **Roquefort cheese → methyl ketones (2-heptanone)**.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Example Solved

**Problem:** A 25 mL sample of yogurt titrates against 0.1 N NaOH and requires 22.5 mL to reach the phenolphthalein endpoint. Initial TA of milk was 0.15 % LA. Find (i) the final % LA, (ii) the developed acidity, and (iii) the equivalent pH (using the empirical relationship).

**Solution:**

**Step (i) — Final TA using the standard formula (adapted for 25 mL).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,LA &= \frac{22.5 \times 0.1 \times 9 \times 100}{25} \\
&= \frac{202.5 \times 100}{25 \times 10} \\
&= 0.81\,\%\,LA
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — careful! The factor 9 is calibrated for **10 mL** sample. For 25 mL, the factor must be rescaled:

$$
\%\,LA = \frac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times 0.9}{V_{sample}} \times 100
$$

Re-evaluating:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,LA &= \frac{22.5 \times 0.1 \times 0.9 \times 100}{25} \\
&= \frac{202.5}{25} \\
&= 0.81\,\%\,LA
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Developed Acidity.**

$$
DA = 0.81 - 0.15 = 0.66\,\%\,LA
$$

**Step (iii) — Equivalent pH.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
pH &\approx 6.20 - 1.50 \times 0.81 \\
&\approx 6.20 - 1.215 \\
&\approx 4.99
\end{aligned}
$$

This pH is close to the expected yogurt end-point (4.4 – 4.6); the slight discrepancy reflects that **TA is not a perfect surrogate for pH** at low buffer capacity.

**Conversion Logic Row:** The 9-factor in the textbook formula is valid for a 10 mL sample. For other volumes, replace **9** with **9 × (10 / $V_{sample}$)**. Many KTU students lose marks by misapplying this constant.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow of Fermented Milk (Yogurt) Manufacture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Milk Reception] --> B[Standardization - 3.5% fat, 8.5% MSNF]
    B --> C[Filtration and Clarification]
    C --> D[Pre-heating 60-65 C]
    D --> E[Homogenization - 15-20 MPa]
    E --> F[Heat Treatment - 90-95 C for 5-10 min]
    F --> G[Cooling to Inoculation Temp - 42-45 C]
    G --> H[Starter Culture Addition - 2-3% DVS or bulk]
    H --> I[Incubation Tank - 42 C, 4-6 h]
    I --> J{End Point Check - pH 4.5 and TA 0.9% LA}
    J -->|No| I
    J -->|Yes| K[Cooling and Quenching - 4-10 C]
    K --> L[Fruit or Flavour Addition - Optional]
    L --> M[Packaging - Cup or Bulk]
    M --> N[Cold Storage - 4 C]
    N --> O[Distribution]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **feedback loop** at Step J mirrors the **pH / TA in-process checkpoint** — without it, over-fermentation produces syneresis (whey separation) and a sharp, unacceptable taste.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fermentation Kinetics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Lactose Substrate] --> E1[beta-galactosidase]
    E1 --> S2[Glucose + Galactose]
    S2 --> P1[EMP Glycolysis]
    P1 --> P2[Pyruvate]
    P2 --> E2[Lactate Dehydrogenase - LDH]
    E2 --> P3[Lactic Acid]
    P3 --> A1[H+ Accumulation]
    A1 --> A2[pH Drop 6.6 to 4.4]
    A2 --> A3[Casein Aggregation at pI 4.6]
    A3 --> A4[Acid Gel - Body and Texture]
    P3 --> A5[TA Rise 0.15 to 0.9 percent LA]
    A2 --> A6[Microbial Inhibition of Pathogens]
    A6 --> A7[Extended Shelf Life]
    S2 --> B1[Minor Pathway - Citrate Metabolism]
    B1 --> B2[Diacetyl and Acetaldehyde]
    B2 --> A8[Characteristic Aroma]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The architecture shows **three parallel output streams** from the central pyruvate node: (1) lactic acid → gel and preservation; (2) acetaldehyde / diacetyl → aroma; (3) H⁺ → microbial inhibition. Each output is independently testable and a likely KTU sub-question.

### 4.3 Microbial Symbiosis Schematic (Thermophilic Pair)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[Streptococcus thermophilus] -->|Produces Formic Acid| Y1[Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus]
    Y1 -->|Releases Peptides and Amino Acids| X1
    X1 -->|Produces CO2 and Acetate| Y1
    Y1 -->|Generates Acetaldehyde| Z1[Yogurt Aroma Profile]
    X1 -->|Generates Lactic Acid| Z2[Acid Gel Formation]
    Y1 -->|Generates Lactic Acid| Z2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q1.** Define *fermented milk*. List **two** thermophilic and **two** mesophilic lactic acid bacteria used in fermented milk manufacture. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- *Definition:* Fermented milk is the product obtained by fermentation of milk by selected lactic acid bacteria, resulting in lactic acid production, acidification (pH drop), coagulation of casein, and the development of characteristic body and flavor.
- *Thermophilic LAB (2):* *Streptococcus thermophilus*, *Lactobacillus delbrueckii* subsp. *bulgaricus*.
- *Mesophilic LAB (2):* *Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis*, *Leuconostoc mesenteroides*.

**Q2.** What is the **role of β-galactosidase** in fermented milk production? Show the stoichiometric equation of lactose-to-lactic acid conversion. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- *Role:* β-galactosidase hydrolyses lactose into glucose and galactose, which then enter the EMP pathway leading to pyruvate and finally lactic acid. Without this enzyme, the LAB cannot metabolize lactose, and fermentation would not proceed.
- *Equation:* $C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} + H_2O \rightarrow 4\,CH_3CHOHCOOH$ (1 mol lactose → 4 mol lactic acid).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

---

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the **industrial manufacture of set-type yogurt** from cow milk. Clearly indicate each unit operation, the **temperature–time** profile, and the **role of starter culture**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **biochemical changes** that occur during yogurt fermentation. How does the symbiotic relationship between *S. thermophilus* and *Lb. bulgaricus* drive fermentation? How is the **end-point** of fermentation determined? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Manufacture of Set-Type Yogurt:**

1. **Raw milk reception and standardization:** Cow milk is standardized to **3.5 % fat** and **8.5 % SNF / MSNF** to obtain a firm, smooth gel. Fortification with skim milk powder (1 – 2 %) is common to raise MSNF.
2. **Filtration and clarification:** Removal of dust, somatic cells, and extraneous matter by centrifugal clarifier.
3. **Pre-heating (60 – 65 °C):** Prepares milk for efficient homogenization.
4. **Homogenization (15 – 20 MPa, two-stage):** Disrupts fat globules → prevents creaming, smooth texture, uniform body.
5. **Heat treatment (90 – 95 °C × 5 – 10 min):**
   - Kills pathogens and spoilage organisms.
   - Denatures whey proteins (β-lactoglobulin) which complex with casein → **firmer, less syneretic gel**.
6. **Cooling to inoculation temperature (42 – 45 °C):** Optimal for thermophilic culture.
7. **Starter culture addition (2 – 3 % DVS):** Mixed culture of *S. thermophilus* and *Lb. delbrueckii* subsp. *bulgaricus* (typical ratio 1:1).
8. **Filling and incubation:** Poured into retail cups; incubated at **42 ± 1 °C for 4 – 6 h**.
9. **End-point check:** pH **4.4 – 4.6** and TA **0.85 – 0.95 % LA**.
10. **Cooling / quenching (4 – 10 °C):** Halts fermentation rapidly, prevents post-acidification.
11. **Optional fruit/flavor addition** (for stirred-type).
12. **Cold storage (4 °C) and distribution.**

**(b) Biochemical Changes & End-Point Determination:**

- **Lactose hydrolysis:** $\beta$-galactosidase cleaves lactose → glucose + galactose.
- **Glycolysis:** EMP pathway generates **2 mol pyruvate per mol glucose** (4 mol per mol lactose).
- **Lactic acid production:** LDH reduces pyruvate to lactic acid; this **lowers pH from 6.6 to ~4.4**.
- **Casein coagulation:** At pH ≈ 5.2, k-casein dissociates; at pI ≈ 4.6, casein micelles aggregate, forming a continuous protein network (gel).
- **Aroma development:** Acetaldehyde (from threonine via *Lb. bulgaricus*) gives the characteristic green-apple yogurt note.
- **Symbiosis:** *S. thermophilus* grows first (shorter lag), produces formic acid that stimulates *Lb. bulgaricus*; the latter releases peptides and amino acids (glycine, histidine) that accelerate streptococcal growth. This proto-cooperation **reduces fermentation time by ~30 %** versus pure cultures.
- **End-point detection methods:**
  1. pH measurement (target 4.4 – 4.6).
  2. Titratable acidity (target 0.85 – 0.95 % LA).
  3. Visual inspection of gel firmness and clarity of whey at cup edge.
  4. Time–temperature integration chart.

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of fermented milks** based on fermentation temperature. List three products under each class along with the **key aroma compound** they generate. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **titratable acidity (TA)**? Derive the formula for the determination of % lactic acid in a fermented milk sample, and compute the % LA if **20 mL of 0.1 N NaOH** is required to titrate **10 mL of yogurt** of density **1.030 g/mL**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Fermented Milks (by temperature):**

- **Mesophilic Fermented Milks (20 – 30 °C):**
  - *Cultured Buttermilk* → Aroma compound: **Diacetyl** (buttery).
  - *Kefir* → Aroma: **Ethanol + CO₂** (slight effervescence from kefir grain yeasts).
  - *Lassi / Dahi (mesophilic variant)* → Aroma: mild lactic; minor diacetyl.

- **Thermophilic Fermented Milks (40 – 45 °C):**
  - *Yogurt* → Aroma compound: **Acetaldehyde** (green-apple).
  - *Bulgarian Milk* → Aroma: enhanced acetaldehyde; firm acidic gel.
  - *Acidophilus Milk* → Aroma: clean lactic; probiotic *Lb. acidophilus*.

**(b) Titratable Acidity (TA) and Computation:**

- *Definition:* TA is the total acid content of a sample (free + undissociated), determined by neutralizing a known volume of sample with a standardized alkali (NaOH) to a phenolphthalein endpoint, expressed in **% lactic acid** equivalent.

- *Derivation:* A volume $V_{sample}$ (mL) of milk is titrated with $V_{NaOH}$ (mL) of $N_{NaOH}$ normality NaOH. Since lactic acid (n = 1) has equivalent weight 90:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Equivalents of NaOH} &= V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \\
\text{Mass of LA} &= V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times 90 \\
\text{Mass of sample} &= V_{sample} \times \rho \\
\%\,LA &= \frac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times 90 \times 100}{V_{sample} \times \rho}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $V_{sample} = 10$ mL and $\rho = 1.030$ g/mL:

$$
\%\,LA = \frac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times 9 \times 100}{V_{sample}}
$$

- *Numerical Computation:* $V_{NaOH} = 20$ mL, $N_{NaOH} = 0.1$, $V_{sample} = 10$ mL.

$$
\%\,LA = \frac{20 \times 0.1 \times 9 \times 100}{10} = \frac{1800}{10} = 1.8\,\%\,LA
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:** Many students write 1.8 % LA as the answer and stop. **However, this is unrealistically high** for yogurt (typical 0.85 – 0.95 % LA). Always **sanity-check** your numerical answer against the practical range. The exam may have used a deliberately inflated value to test whether you apply the formula correctly *and* interpret results. A good answer also remarks: *"In practice, 1.8 % LA is far above commercial yogurt range — this may suggest a low-quality or over-fermented sample, or a procedural error in dilution/sample volume."*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE (Module 1 — Fermented Milk):**

- **Definition:** Fermented milk = milk + LAB + lactic acid + (optional) aroma compounds.
- **Stoichiometry (must memorize):** $C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} + H_2O \rightarrow 4\,CH_3CHOHCOOH$; yield factor = **1.053 g LA / g lactose**.
- **Microbes — Thermophilic:** *S. thermophilus* + *Lb. delbrueckii* subsp. *bulgaricus* (Yogurt).
- **Microbes — Mesophilic:** *Lactococcus lactis* + *Lc. cremoris* + *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* (Buttermilk).
- **Aroma Map:** Yogurt → **Acetaldehyde**; Butter-milk → **Diacetyl**; Kefir → **Ethanol + CO₂**; Cheese (Roquefort) → **2-Heptanone**.
- **Symbiosis:** Formic acid ↔ peptides (the "*S. thermophilus* ⇆ *Lb. bulgaricus*" loop).
- **Critical Process Parameters:**
  - Heat treatment **90 – 95 °C × 5 – 10 min** (denatures whey proteins).
  - Homogenization at **15 – 20 MPa** (two-stage).
  - Inoculation at **42 – 45 °C** (thermophilic) or **20 – 30 °C** (mesophilic).
  - Incubation time **4 – 6 h** (yogurt), **14 – 16 h** (buttermilk).
- **End-point of Fermentation:** pH **4.4 – 4.6**, TA **0.85 – 0.95 % LA** for yogurt.
- **Casein Isoelectric Point:** **4.6** (coagulation begins at ~5.2).
- **Titratable Acidity Formula (10 mL sample, ρ = 1.030):**

$$
\%\,LA = \frac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times 9 \times 100}{V_{sample}}
$$

- **Codex Standards (for 14-mark answers):** Min. **0.6 % LA**, Min. **2.7 % milk protein** for Codex fermented milks.
- **Post-acidification:** Continuous slow acid production during cold storage is normal; thermophilic cultures are more active in this regard than mesophilic.
- **Syneresis:** Whey separation is a **defect** indicating poor gel structure — caused by low MSNF, over-incubation, or rough handling.
- **Industrial Yields (η):** Lactic acid from lactose at full fermentation ≈ **85 – 95 %** of theoretical.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Liner for Last-Minute Recall:** *"Lactose + Water → 4 Lactic Acid; 1 g lactose ≈ 1.053 g LA; yogurt pH ≈ 4.5; TA ≈ 0.9 %; Acetaldehyde smells; Diacetyl tastes buttery; Kefir fizzes."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cheese
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT412 - FOOD PROCESS TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cheese # 🧀 Cheese — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cheese** is a concentrated dairy product obtained by the **coagulation of the casein fraction of milk**, accompanied by the partial removal of water, water-soluble constituents (mainly lactose, whey proteins, minerals, and vitamins), and subsequent ripening (curing/aging) of the curd under controlled environmental conditions to develop characteristic flavor, texture, and body. According to **Codex Alimentarius (FAO/WHO Standard A-6-1978, revised)**, the term *"cheese"* applies exclusively to the ripened or unripened **curd** obtained by coagulating milk, skimmed milk, partially skimmed milk, cream, whey cream, or buttermilk — through the action of **rennet** (or other suitable coagulating enzymes), **acid** (lactic or mineral), or a **combination of both**. The minimum milk-solids-not-fat (MSNF) content in the finished cheese, expressed as **moisture on a fat-free basis (MFFB)**, is the regulated index that defines cheese identity (e.g., hard, semi-hard, soft). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Sponge" Intuition Think of milk as a **giant swimming pool** where casein proteins are tiny balloons floating around, kept apart from each other by a soapy layer called **κ-casein** (kappa-casein). - When you add **rennet (enzyme)**, it's like **popping the safety pin on every balloon** — the balloons (caseins) collapse together and form a continuous **gel network** (the curd). - When you add **lactic acid (from bacteria or vinegar)**, it's like **squeezing the sponge hard** — water (whey) is forced out of the structure. - The solid part left behind is the **curd**, the liquid expelled is the **whey**, and the entire journey of transforming, salting, pressing, and aging this curd is what gives us the **cheese** on our pizza 🍕. ## 1.3 Classification Snapshot (Standard KTU Reference) | Basis | Categories | |---|---| | **Texture (Moisture %)** | Hard (≤ 35%), Semi-hard (36–45%), Semi-soft (46–55%), Soft (≥ 56%) | | **Coagulation Method** | Rennet cheese, Acid cheese, Acid-rennet cheese | | **Ripening** | Unripened (Fresh), Ripened | | **Milk Origin** | Cow, Buffalo, Goat, Sheep, Mixed | | **Manufacturing Style** | Natural cheese, Processed (cheese food, cheese spread) | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric:** Cheese yield in commercial practice is reported in **kg of cheese per 100 kg of standardized milk**, with a typical Cheddar yield of **~10 kg / 100 kg** (10% v/v basis). --- # 📚 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Chemical Composition of Milk — The Raw Substrate Milk (whole, bovine) used in cheese making contains approximately: - **Water:** ~ 87.0 % (w/w) - **Fat (Lipid globules):** ~ 4.0 % - **Casein proteins (αs1, αs2, β, κ):** ~ 2.6 % — the **curd-former** - **Whey proteins (β-lactoglobulin, α-lactalbumin):** ~ 0.6 % — lost in whey - **Lactose (disaccharide sugar):** ~ 4.8 % — substrate for lactic starter - **Minerals (Ca, P, K, Na, Mg, Cl):** ~ 0.7 % - **Vitamins (A, D, B2, B12):** trace > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Focal Point:** Of the total milk protein (~3.2 %), **casein constitutes ~80 %** and **whey proteins ~20 %**. Only **casein** is retained in cheese; whey proteins are the primary nitrogenous loss. ## 2.2 Stages of Cheese Manufacture — The 11-Step Master Pipeline 1. **Standardization of milk** — adjusting the fat : casein ratio (commonly 1 : 0.7 for Cheddar) to control cheese composition and meet regulatory fat-in-dry-matter (FDM) limits. 2. **Pasteurization** — usually **72 °C for 15 s** (HTST, High-Temperature Short-Time) to eliminate pathogens and spoilage microbes while preserving most enzymes. 3. **Cooling & Ripening** — milk cooled to **~ 30 °C** (mesophilic) or **~ 39 °C** (thermophilic); starter culture added at ~ 1–2 %. 4. **Acid Development (Pre-ripening)** — lactic acid bacteria ferment lactose to lactic acid; pH drops to **~ 6.5**. 5. **Coagulation** — rennet added at typical strength of **~ 20–25 mL/100 kg milk** (single-strength rennet), sets curd in ~ 30 min. 6. **Cutting of Curd** — vertical and horizontal wire knives cut gel into **5–15 mm cubes**, increasing surface area for syneresis. 7. **Cooking / Stirring** — curd-whey mixture gradually heated (e.g., 38–42 °C for Cheddar) to expel more whey. 8. **Draining of Whey** — whey is removed; curd is *"cheddared"* by matting and turning. 9. **Milling, Salting, Hooping & Pressing** — curd milled, dry-salted (~ 2–3 % by weight of curd), filled into hoops, pressed at **~ 0.5–1.5 bar**. 10. **Salting (Brine / Dry)** — adjusts moisture, controls surface microflora, enhances rind. 11. **Ripening / Aging** — stored at **8–15 °C** and **80–90 % RH** for periods ranging from *0 days* (Mozzarella, Paneer) to **> 24 months** (Parmigiano-Reggiano). ## 2.3 Mechanism of Rennet Coagulation — *The Molecular Story* - **Phase I — Enzymatic:** Chymosin (rennin) cleaves the **Phe105 – Met106** peptide bond of **κ-casein**, releasing the hydrophilic **caseinomacropeptide (CMP)** into whey. - **Phase II — Aggregation:** Destabilized casein micelles (now coated only with hydrophobic para-κ-casein) aggregate via **Ca²⁺ bridges** to form a continuous three-dimensional protein network (gel). - This gel traps fat, water, and water-soluble solids → **the curd.** ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Expression | Standard Unit | Typical Value / Range | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Cheese Yield (Van Slyke & Pubols, 1925)** | $Y = \dfrac{0.93\,(F - 0.06) + (C - 0.10)\,S}{1 - W}$ | kg cheese / 100 kg milk | 8–12 kg | | 2 | **Moisture on Fat-Free Basis (MFFB)** | $\text{MFFB} = \dfrac{M \times 100}{100 - F}$ | % (w/w) | Hard: ≤ 51, Soft: ≥ 61 | | 3 | **Fat on Dry Matter (FDM)** | $\text{FDM} = \dfrac{F \times 100}{100 - M}$ | % (w/w) | Cheddar: ≥ 48, Cream: ≥ 60 | | 4 | **Salt-in-Moisture (S/M)** | $\text{S/M} = \dfrac{S \times 100}{M}$ | % (w/w) | Cheddar: 4 – 6 | | 5 | **Casein Recovery Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{casein}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{cheese}}}{C_{\text{milk}}} \times 100$ | % | 75 – 92 | | 6 | **Fat Recovery Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{fat}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{cheese}}}{F_{\text{milk}}} \times 100$ | % | 80 – 95 | | 7 | **Titratable Acidity** | $\text{TA} = \dfrac{V_{\text{NaOH}} \times N_{\text{NaOH}}}{V_{\text{sample}}} \times 100$ | % lactic acid | Fresh milk: 0.13 – 0.16 | | 8 | **pH at Moulding (Cheddar)** | empirical | pH | 6.0 – 6.1 | | 9 | **Syneresis rate (pseudo-first order)** | $M_t = M_{\infty} - (M_{\infty} - M_0)e^{-k t}$ | fraction | $k \approx 0.05 \,\text{min}^{-1}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Notation key for formulas:** $Y$ = cheese yield (% by wt.), $F$ = fat % in milk, $C$ = casein % in milk, $S$ = salt % in cheese, $W$ = moisture % in cheese, $M$ = moisture %, $F$ = fat % in cheese. Verify context for each formula use. ## 2.5 Engineering & Industrial Utility - **Dairy Industry:** Cheese plants form the **second-largest segment** of the processed dairy economy globally, after liquid milk. - **Co-Product Engineering:** Whey — once considered waste — is now valorized into **Whey Protein Concentrate (WPC)**, **Whey Protein Isolate (WPI)**, **lactose powder**, and **bioactive peptides** through membrane and chromatographic separations. - **Food Process Engineering Design:** Cheese yield equations are the foundational design criterion for **vat sizing, rennet procurement, starter culture preparation, and quality-control analytical panels**. --- # 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Van Slyke Cheese Yield Equation (Simplified Mass-Balance Form) **Starting Premise:** The mass of cheese produced per 100 kg of milk can be expressed as the sum of fat, casein, and the aqueous phase remaining after whey expulsion. Loss factors for fat (lost to whey as fat globules and as free fat) and casein (lost as fine curd particles through strainers) must be incorporated. **Step 1 — Define baseline component retention:** Assume milk with fat fraction $F$ (%) and casein fraction $C$ (%). The available curd solids are: $$M_{\text{available}} = F + C \quad \text{(per 100 kg milk, ignoring lactose/minerals initially)}$$ **Step 2 — Apply empirical loss factors (Van Slyke's regression, calibrated with US cheddar data):** Fat recovery in cheese $\approx 93\,\%$ (so loss $\approx 0.06\,F$). Casein recovery in cheese $\approx 90\,\%$ (so loss $\approx 0.10\,C$). Retained curd solids: $$M_{\text{retained}} = 0.93\,F + 0.90\,C$$ **Step 3 — Introduce salt correction:** Adding $S$ (% of cheese weight) raises total cheese mass: $$M_{\text{retained+salt}} = 0.93\,F + 0.90\,C + S$$ Subtract the small (empirically adjusted) loss constants used in the original equation: $$M_{\text{retained+salt}} - \text{constants} \;\longrightarrow\; 0.93(F - 0.06) + (C - 0.10)S$$ **Step 4 — Normalize for moisture content in cheese:** If cheese is $W$ % moisture, then solids = $(1 - W/100)$ fraction. Cheese mass is therefore: $$Y = \dfrac{0.93(F - 0.06) + (C - 0.10)S}{1 - W}$$ This is the canonical **Van Slyke & Pubols (1925) yield formula**, still used in dairy industry mass-balance auditing. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Cheddar Style) **Given:** Milk with $F = 3.5\,\%$, $C = 2.5\,\%$, salt $S = 2.0\,\%$, target cheese moisture $W = 36\,\%$. Estimate cheese yield. **Step 1 — Substitute into numerator:** $$N = 0.93 \times (3.5 - 0.06) + (2.5 - 0.10) \times 2.0$$ **Step 2 — Evaluate term-by-term:** $$N = 0.93 \times 3.44 + 2.40 \times 2.0$$ $$N = 3.199 + 4.800 = 7.999$$ **Step 3 — Compute denominator:** $$D = 1 - \dfrac{36}{100} = 0.64$$ **Step 4 — Final yield:** $$Y = \dfrac{7.999}{0.64} \approx 12.50 \;\text{kg cheese per 100 kg milk}$$ This matches the **empirically observed Cheddar yield** (10.5–12.5 kg/100 kg) from Western dairy literature. ## 3.3 MFFB — Worked Example **Given:** A semi-hard cheese with $M = 40\,\%$, $F = 28\,\%$. Compute MFFB. $$\text{MFFB} = \dfrac{40 \times 100}{100 - 28} = \dfrac{4000}{72} \approx 55.6\,\%$$ Classification: since MFFB lies in 51–61 % range → **semi-hard cheese** (e.g., Gouda, Havarti). ## 3.4 Fat Recovery Efficiency — Worked Example **Given:** Milk with $F_{\text{milk}} = 3.5\,\%$, Cheese with $F_{\text{cheese}} = 32\,\%$ and 100 kg milk yields 11 kg cheese. **Step 1 — Fat entering cheese:** $$F_{\text{cheese (kg)}} = \dfrac{32 \times 11}{100} = 3.52\;\text{kg}$$ **Step 2 — Fat entering from milk:** $$F_{\text{milk (kg)}} = \dfrac{3.5 \times 100}{100} = 3.50\;\text{kg}$$ **Step 3 — Apply efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{fat}} = \dfrac{3.52}{3.50} \times 100 \approx 100.6\,\%$$ A value slightly above 100 % suggests concentration correction — an apparent > 100 % arises because cheese milk is **standardized to a slightly higher fat level** than the raw whole milk. For a realistic plant where standardization is exact: $$\eta_{\text{fat, real}} \approx 88 - 92\,\%$$ --- # 🏗️ Structural Diagram — Cheese Manufacture Block-Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Milk Reception] --> B[Standardization
Fat to Casein Ratio Adjustment] B --> C[HTST Pasteurization
72C for 15 s] C --> D[Cooling to Inoculation Temp
30C Mesophilic or 39C Thermophilic] D --> E[Starter Culture Addition
Lactococcus or Streptococcus 1 to 2 percent] E --> F[Acid Development
pH drops to 6.5] F --> G[Rennet Addition
Chymosin 20 mL per 100 kg] G --> H[Coagulation Set
30 min for firm gel] H --> I[Cutting of Curd
5 to 15 mm cubes] I --> J[Cooking and Stirring
38 to 42 C syneresis] J --> K[Whey Drainage
Whey to recovery plant] K --> L[Cheddaring
Matting and turning] L --> M[Milling and Dry Salting
2 to 3 percent NaCl] M --> N[Hooping and Pressing
0.5 to 1.5 bar] N --> O[Brine Salting Optional] O --> P[Ripening or Aging
8 to 15 C, 80 to 90 percent RH] P --> Q[Packaging and Cold Storage
4 to 6 C] style A fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600 style C fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600 style G fill:#ff9966,stroke:#993300,color:#ffffff style K fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366,color:#003366 style P fill:#d9b3ff,stroke:#330099,color:#ffffff style Q fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600 ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The **left-side stream (A → I)** constitutes *"green cheese"* formation. - The **middle band (J → M)** is where moisture, salt, and acid balance are established. - The **right-side loop (N → Q)** is the **ripening phase** where proteolysis, lipolysis, and glycolysis drive flavor and texture development. > [!NOTE] > For **unripened / fresh cheeses** (Paneer, Ricotta, Mozzarella—fresh form), the ripening block (P) is **bypassed**, and packaging is performed directly after pressing and salting. --- # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define cheese. List any four physical characteristics used to classify cheese in the dairy industry.** **Model Answer:** Cheese is the ripened or unripened curd obtained by coagulation of milk, skimmed milk, or cream, through the action of rennet, lactic acid, or a combination, followed by partial removal of whey. Four physical characteristics used for classification: 1. **Moisture content (M %)** — primary index. 2. **Moisture on fat-free basis (MFFB)** — regulatory index. 3. **Fat on dry matter (FDM)** — defines cheese family (e.g., double cream, full fat). 4. **Texture (hardness/smoothness)** — empirically evaluated by rheology and bite. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is rennet? Name its active enzyme and the specific bond it hydrolyzes in casein.** **Model Answer:** Rennet is a commercial enzymatic preparation traditionally extracted from the abomasum (4th stomach) of unweaned calves. Its **active enzyme is chymosin (EC 3.4.23.4)**, also called **rennin**. Chymosin specifically cleaves the **phenylalanine-105 ↔ methionine-106** peptide bond of **κ-casein**, releasing the **caseinomacropeptide (CMP, residues 106–169)** into the whey and leaving **para-κ-casein** anchored to the micelle. This destabilizes the micelle and triggers casein aggregation. --- ## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Solve both sub-parts in the answer book in sequence. --- ### ❓ Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Describe in detail the **principal steps involved in the manufacture of Cheddar cheese**, with a labelled flow diagram. **(b)** Explain the **Van Slyke yield formula** and use it to estimate yield for milk of $F = 4.0\,\%$, $C = 2.6\,\%$, $S = 1.8\,\%$, and cheese moisture $W = 38\,\%$. #### Model Solution: **(a) Manufacture of Cheddar Cheese (7 marks):** Cheddar is a **hard, ripened cheese** of English origin. The key steps are: 1. **Standardization** of whole milk to a Casein : Fat ratio of approximately **0.7 : 1** to comply with FDM ≥ 48 %. 2. **Pasteurization** by HTST (72 °C / 15 s). 3. **Cooling to 30 °C** and addition of **mesophilic starter** (Lactococcus lactis subsp. lactis + L. lactis subsp. cremoris at 1–2 %). 4. **Ripening for 30–45 min** until titratable acidity rises by ~ 0.02 %. 5. **Rennet addition** (~ 22 mL/100 kg) — coagulation in **~ 30 min** to a clean break. 6. **Cutting** into 8–10 mm cubes. 7. **Cooking** by raising temperature gradually from 30 °C to **38–39 °C** over 30 min with continuous stirring; this expels whey (syneresis). 8. **Whey drainage** when TA reaches ~ 0.18 %. 9. **Cheddaring** — curd mats are piled, turned every 15 min; TA reaches ~ 0.45 %, pH drops to 5.4–5.6. 10. **Milling** — curd passed through mill to obtain uniform finger-sized pieces. 11. **Dry salting** — 2.5 % NaCl mixed into milled curd. 12. **Hooping & pressing** in cheese hoops at ~ 1 bar for 18–24 h. 13. **Aging** at **8–10 °C** for 3–9 months; periodic rind control. (Diagram: see SECTION 4 of these notes.) **(b) Van Slyke Yield Estimation (7 marks):** $$Y = \dfrac{0.93\,(F - 0.06) + (C - 0.10)\,S}{1 - W}$$ Substituting $F = 4.0$, $C = 2.6$, $S = 1.8$, $W = 38$: **Step 1 — Numerator computation:** $$N = 0.93\,(4.0 - 0.06) + (2.6 - 0.10) \times 1.8$$ $$N = 0.93 \times 3.94 + 2.50 \times 1.8$$ $$N = 3.6642 + 4.500 = 8.1642$$ **Step 2 — Denominator:** $$D = 1 - \dfrac{38}{100} = 0.62$$ **Step 3 — Final yield:** $$Y = \dfrac{8.1642}{0.62} \approx 13.17\;\text{kg cheese / 100 kg milk}$$ This is consistent with standardized, full-fat Cheddar manufacture. --- ### ❓ Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Differentiate between **acid-coagulated cheeses** and **rennet-coagulated cheeses** with suitable examples for each. **(b)** Explain the concepts of **MFFB, FDM, and Salt-in-Moisture (S/M)** indices used in cheese quality control. #### Model Solution: **(a) Acid vs. Rennet-Coagulated Cheeses (7 marks):** | Feature | Acid-Coagulated Cheese | Rennet-Coagulated Cheese | |---|---|---| | **Coagulating Agent** | Lactic acid (or vinegar, citric acid, glucono-δ-lactone) | Chymosin (rennet) or microbial coagulants | | **Mechanism** | Casein destabilization at isoelectric pH (~ 4.6) | Specific κ-casein hydrolysis + Ca²⁺ bridging | | **Curd Texture** | Soft, fragile, fragile gel | Firm, elastic, cuttable | | **Calcium Content** | Low (calcium lost during acidification) | High (calcium retained with curd) | | **Shelf Life** | Short (1–3 weeks refrigerated) | Long (weeks to years with ripening) | | **Examples** | Paneer, Cottage cheese, Quark, Cream cheese | Cheddar, Gouda, Parmesan, Swiss, Mozzarella | | **Yield** | Low (~ 6–10 kg/100 kg) | High (~ 10–13 kg/100 kg) | **(b) Quality Control Indices (7 marks):** **1. Moisture on Fat-Free Basis (MFFB):** $$\text{MFFB} = \dfrac{M}{100 - F} \times 100$$ It represents the moisture in the **non-fat portion** of the cheese. Regulatory agencies use MFFB because moisture expressed as a percentage of total cheese mass is confounded by fat content. FSSAI (India) and Codex specify MFFB maxima for cheese identity. **2. Fat on Dry Matter (FDM):** $$\text{FDM} = \dfrac{F}{100 - M} \times 100$$ It expresses fat as a percentage of total solids. FDM defines commercial cheese categories: - **Double cream cheese:** FDM ≥ 60 % - **Full fat cheese:** FDM ≥ 45 % - **Medium fat:** FDM ≥ 25 % and < 45 % - **Skim milk cheese:** FDM < 10 % **3. Salt-in-Moisture (S/M):** $$\text{S/M} = \dfrac{S}{M} \times 100$$ It governs **water activity (a_w)**, microbial stability, and rate of ripening. Typical S/M values: - Cheddar: 4–6 % — moderate - Gouda: 5–7 % - Cottage cheese (salted): 1.0–1.5 % — low (fresh consumption) --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Don't confuse moisture (% of cheese mass) with MFFB (% of fat-free mass).** Examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks when students mix these up. > 2. **The Van Slyke formula constants (0.06, 0.10) are calibration constants for fat and casein loss respectively — NOT universal!** They are tuned to Cheddar-style manufacture. State this assumption. > 3. **Rennet ≠ rennin.** Rennet is the commercial preparation; **rennin (chymosin)** is the enzyme. A common 1-mark deduction. > 4. **κ-casein bond:** Always write **Phe105-Met106**. Do not invent bonds like "Phe-105-Met-105" — students lose marks for incorrect residue numbers. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember - **Cheese = concentrated curd of milk**, obtained by coagulation and whey expulsion. - **Milk casein (~2.6 %)** is the curd-forming protein; **whey proteins (~0.6 %)** are lost in whey. - **Chymosin (rennin)** in rennet cleaves the **Phe105–Met106 bond of κ-casein** → releases **caseinomacropeptide (CMP)**. - **Three coagulation types:** acid, rennet, acid-rennet (mixed). - **Eleven canonical process steps** from standardization to ripening. - **Yield equation (Van Slyke):** $Y = \dfrac{0.93(F - 0.06) + (C - 0.10)S}{1 - W}$. - **MFFB** = moisture on fat-free basis = $M \times 100 / (100 - F)$ — defines texture class. - **FDM** = fat on dry matter = $F \times 100 / (100 - M)$ — defines commercial category. - **Salt-in-Moisture (S/M)** = $S \times 100 / M$ — governs $a_w$, microbial safety, ripening rate. - **Cheddar is hard cheese** (MFFB ≤ 51 %), **Mozzarella is semi-soft** (MFFB 52–60 %), **Paneer / Ricotta are unripened soft cheeses.** - **Whey is a valuable co-product** — source of WPC, WPI, lactose, bioactive peptides. - **Ripening** = biochemical maturation via **proteolysis, glycolysis, lipolysis** by starter and non-starter microflora. - **Standard process parameters to memorize:** HTST = 72 °C / 15 s; Cheddar cook temp = 38–39 °C; aging temp = 8–10 °C; rennet dose ≈ 20–25 mL/100 kg milk. ---

topics.title: Butter
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT412 - FOOD PROCESS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Butter

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Butter** is a water-in-oil (W/O) emulsion derived exclusively from milk, cream, or both, obtained through the physical separation and concentration of the fat phase. As per the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011 (FSSAI)**, butter is legally defined by the following minimum compositional standards:

- **Milk Fat:** Not less than **80% by weight**
- **Moisture:** Not more than **16% by weight**
- **Milk Solids Not Fat (SNF):** Not more than **3% by weight**
- **Salt (for salted butter):** Not more than **3% by weight**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Butter is fundamentally a **fat-continuous (W/O) emulsion** where discrete water droplets containing milk solids, salt, and air bubbles are dispersed in a continuous fat phase stabilized by fat globule membranes. This emulsion inversion (from O/W in cream to W/O in butter) is the central physico-chemical concept behind butter manufacturing.

### Legal Classification (FSSAI 2011)

| Product Variant | Min. Fat (%) | Max. Moisture (%) | Max. SNF (%) | Max. Salt (%) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Table Butter | 80 | 16 | 3 | 3 (if salted) |
| White Butter | 80 | 16 | 3 | - |
| Cooking Butter | 76 | 18 | 3 | 3 |
| Ghee (Clarified) | 99.5 | 0.5 | 0.5 | - |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 80% Fat?** The 80% fat threshold represents the critical inversion point where the emulsion transitions from a **fat-in-water (cream)** system to a **water-in-fat (butter)** system. Below this fat concentration, the product is classified as "Margarine," "Low-fat spread," or "Dairy spread."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **kitchen sponge soaked in oil**:
- The **sponge itself** represents the **fat phase** (continuous matrix).
- The **water trapped inside the sponge's pores** represents the **dispersed water droplets** (containing dissolved lactose, minerals, and milk proteins).
- The **wringing action** represents the **churning process** that expels excess water (buttermilk).

**Real-World Analogy — The Shaking Cream Experiment:**
When you vigorously shake a bottle of fresh cream, you are physically performing the industrial "churning" process. The mechanical agitation destabilizes the **fat globule membranes** (originally stabilized by milk proteins like casein), causing the fat globules to **clump together (flocculation → coalescence)**, separating from the watery phase (buttermilk) and forming a semi-solid mass — **butter**.

> [!TIP]
> **First-time Learner Insight:** The transformation from liquid cream → solid butter is NOT a chemical change. It is a **physical emulsion inversion** triggered by mechanical shear and temperature control.

---

## 1.3 Composition of Butter (Typical)

The approximate composition of a standard table butter is:

- **Milk Fat:** **80–82%** (triglycerides, phospholipids, sterols, fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, K, β-carotene)
- **Water/Moisture:** **15.5–17%** (containing dissolved lactose, whey proteins, mineral salts)
- **SNF (Milk Solids Not Fat):** **1.0–2.0%** (casein, whey proteins, lactose, ash)
- **Salt:** **0–3%** (NaCl, added for flavor, preservation, and shelf life extension)
- **Air/N₂ (occluded):** **1–5% by volume** (incorporated during churning)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **β-Carotene** is responsible for the natural **yellow color** of butter. It is a fat-soluble pigment present in cow feed (grass and hay). The color intensity is highest in summer (when cows graze on fresh grass) and lighter in winter (silage-fed cows).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physico-Chemical Principles of Butter Formation

Butter manufacturing relies on **three critical physico-chemical transitions**:

### A. Fat Globule Destabilization
Fresh milk fat exists as **globules (0.1–10 µm diameter)** surrounded by a protective **Milk Fat Globule Membrane (MFGM)**. The MFGM is composed of phospholipids, proteins (glycoproteins, butyrophilin), and enzymes. The MFGM acts as a natural **emulsifier**, keeping fat dispersed in the aqueous milk phase (O/W emulsion).

### B. Phase Inversion (Cream → Butter)
During churning, mechanical shear energy:
1. **Disrupts the MFGM** through fat globule collision and shear stress.
2. **Promotes partial coalescence** — fat globules stick together without fully merging, forming a 3D interconnected network of fat clusters.
3. At a critical fat concentration (~**80%**), the emulsion **inverts** from O/W to W/O, releasing buttermilk.

### C. Plasticity (Workability at Refrigeration Temperature)
Properly worked butter exhibits **plastic rheology** — soft enough to spread at **4–10 °C** yet firm enough to hold its shape. This is achieved by controlling the **solid fat content (SFC)** through **tempering (15–20 °C holding)** to crystallize the triglycerides in the β'-polymorphic form.

---

## 2.2 Raw Material: Cream and its Standardization

**Cream** is the primary raw material. It is standardized to a target fat content (typically **35–40% fat** for batch churning; **40–45%** for continuous processes) to optimize churning efficiency and yield.

### Cream Pasteurization
Cream is **heat-treated** to:
- **Destroy pathogens** (Listeria, Salmonella, Mycobacterium tuberculosis).
- **Inactivate lipases** (enzymes that hydrolyze triglycerides → free fatty acids → rancidity).
- **Create a uniform viscosity** for consistent churning.

> Standard pasteurization protocols: **HTST (72 °C / 15 sec)** for table butter, or **95 °C / 5 sec** for enhanced microbial kill in cultured butter.

### Cream Aging (Cool Maturation)
Post-pasteurization, cream is rapidly cooled to **4–6 °C** and held for **12–24 hours**. Aging promotes:
- **Crystallization of high-melting triglycerides** → ensures proper solid fat content for butter texture.
- **Viscosity increase** → promotes efficient churning.
- **Lipid re-association** of fat globule components.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cultured Butter** undergoes an additional **fermentation step** using mesophilic starter cultures (*Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis*, *L. lactis* subsp. *cremoris*, *Leuconostoc mesenteroides*) at 18–22 °C for 16–18 hours. This generates **lactic acid** (pH 4.6–4.8) and **diacetyl** (the characteristic butter aroma compound).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Equations for KTU Numerical Problems** (Use these for any yield, balance, or standardization calculation)

### Table 1: Master Formula Reference

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $$Y_{butter} = \frac{M_{cream} \times F_{cream}}{F_{butter}}$$ | Butter Yield from Cream | kg |
| 2 | $$F_{butter} = \frac{M_{fat}}{M_{butter}} \times 100$$ | Fat % in Butter | % w/w |
| 3 | $$\eta_{recovery} = \frac{M_{fat, butter}}{M_{fat, cream}} \times 100$$ | Fat Recovery Efficiency | % |
| 4 | $$V_{buttermilk} = V_{cream} \times \left(1 - \frac{F_{cream}}{F_{butter}}\right)$$ | Theoretical Buttermilk Volume | L |
| 5 | $$\Delta pH = pH_{initial} - pH_{final}$$ | Fermentation Acidity Drop | dimensionless |
| 6 | $$E_{churn} = \frac{V_{cream}}{t_{churn}}$$ | Churner Throughput Efficiency | L/min |
| 7 | $SFC = f(T)$ | Solid Fat Content (temperature-dependent) | % w/w |

**Where the variables denote:**
- $Y_{butter}$ = Mass of butter produced
- $M_{cream}, M_{fat}, M_{butter}$ = Mass of cream, fat, and butter respectively
- $F_{cream}, F_{butter}$ = Fat percentage in cream and butter
- $V_{cream}, V_{buttermilk}$ = Volume of cream and buttermilk
- $t_{churn}$ = Churning time
- $T$ = Temperature of tempering

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Markdown Conflict Avoidance:** The standard KTU notation for absolute values and ranges uses `\vert ... \vert` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) and never the bare pipe character `\vert` to preserve table formatting integrity.

---

## 2.4 Process Flow Architecture

The industrial butter manufacturing follows this structured sequence:

1. **Reception & Clarification** of raw milk
2. **Cream Separation** (centrifugal cream separator)
3. **Standardization** of cream fat content
4. **Pasteurization** (HTST or UHT)
5. **Vacuum De-aeration** (optional, for cultured butter)
6. **Cooling and Aging** (4–6 °C, 12–24 h)
7. **Inoculation with Starter Culture** (for cultured butter only)
8. **Churning** (Phase inversion: O/W → W/O)
9. **Buttermilk Drainage**
10. **Washing** (to remove lactose and curd particles)
11. **Salting** (dry salt or brine)
12. **Working/Kneading** (uniform moisture droplet distribution)
13. **Packaging** (foil-laminate, parchment, or tubs)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Continuous butter makers (e.g., **Westfalia, Tetra Pak, GEA**) process **5,000–25,000 L/h of cream**, producing butter in less than **2 minutes** from cream entry to butter exit, replacing the traditional batch churn (6–45 min cycle). Continuous processes are essential in the **economies of scale** dominating modern dairy industry (Amul, Mother Dairy, Arla, Fonterra plants).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative 1: Butter Yield from Cream

### Problem Statement
A dairy plant processes **1,000 kg of 40% fat cream** into butter containing **82% fat**. The fat recovery efficiency is **98.5%**. Calculate the **mass of butter produced** and the **volume of buttermilk** generated (assuming density of buttermilk ≈ **1.03 g/mL**).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate the total mass of fat available in the cream.

$$M_{fat, cream} = M_{cream} \times \frac{F_{cream}}{100}$$

$$M_{fat, cream} = 1000 \text{ kg} \times \frac{40}{100} = 400 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the fat recovery efficiency to find actual fat retained in the butter.

$$M_{fat, butter} = M_{fat, cream} \times \frac{\eta_{recovery}}{100}$$

$$M_{fat, butter} = 400 \text{ kg} \times \frac{98.5}{100} = 394 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the mass of butter produced using the inverse of the fat percentage formula.

$$M_{butter} = \frac{M_{fat, butter}}{F_{butter}} \times 100$$

$$M_{butter} = \frac{394 \text{ kg}}{82} \times 100 = 480.49 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the mass of buttermilk produced using mass balance.

$$M_{buttermilk} = M_{cream} - M_{butter}$$

$$M_{buttermilk} = 1000 \text{ kg} - 480.49 \text{ kg} = 519.51 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5:** Convert mass of buttermilk to volume using density.

$$V_{buttermilk} = \frac{M_{buttermilk}}{\rho_{buttermilk}}$$

$$V_{buttermilk} = \frac{519.51 \text{ kg}}{1.03 \text{ kg/L}} = 504.38 \text{ L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** The plant will produce **~480.5 kg of butter** and **~504.4 L of buttermilk** from **1,000 kg of 40% fat cream**.

---

## 3.2 Derivative 2: Cream Standardization Calculation

### Problem Statement
A dairy plant has access to **two cream streams**:
- **Stream A:** 3,000 kg of **30% fat** cream (from light separation)
- **Stream B:** 1,000 kg of **45% fat** cream (heavy cream)

The plant wants to produce a **standardized cream of 38% fat** to be used as the churning feed. How much **whole milk (3.5% fat)** must be added to the **blended cream** to achieve the target fat percentage?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate fat contributed by Stream A.

$$F_A = 3000 \text{ kg} \times 0.30 = 900 \text{ kg fat}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate fat contributed by Stream B.

$$F_B = 1000 \text{ kg} \times 0.45 = 450 \text{ kg fat}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate total mass and total fat of the blended cream.

$$M_{blend} = M_A + M_B = 3000 + 1000 = 4000 \text{ kg}$$

$$F_{blend} = F_A + F_B = 900 + 450 = 1350 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the fat % of the current blend.

$$F_{\% , blend} = \frac{1350}{4000} \times 100 = 33.75\%$$

**Step 5:** Apply the **Pearson Square (alligation) method** to find the ratio of blend to whole milk.

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{F_{target} - F_{blend}}{F_{target} - F_{milk}}$$

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{38 - 33.75}{38 - 3.5} = \frac{4.25}{34.5} = 0.1232$$

This means for every **1 kg of 33.75% blend**, we need **0.1232 kg of 3.5% whole milk**.

**Step 6:** Calculate the mass of whole milk required.

$$M_{milk} = 4000 \text{ kg} \times 0.1232 = 492.75 \text{ kg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Approximately **493 kg of whole milk (3.5% fat)** must be added to the **4,000 kg blended cream (33.75% fat)** to produce a standardized churning feed of **38% fat** (total mass: **4,492.75 kg**).

---

## 3.3 Derivative 3: Salt Calculation for Salted Butter

### Problem Statement
A manufacturer wants to produce **500 kg of salted butter** containing **2.0% NaCl**. If the salt is added as a **dry salt** (assume **100% salt purity, no moisture**), calculate:
- **(a)** Mass of dry salt required
- **(b)** Mass of unsalted butter base

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step (a):** Calculate mass of dry salt.

$$M_{salt} = M_{butter, total} \times \frac{\%_{salt}}{100}$$

$$M_{salt} = 500 \text{ kg} \times 0.020 = 10 \text{ kg salt}$$

**Step (b):** Calculate mass of unsalted butter base.

$$M_{unsalted} = M_{butter, total} - M_{salt}$$

$$M_{unsalted} = 500 \text{ kg} - 10 \text{ kg} = 490 \text{ kg}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Note:** In commercial practice, salt is typically added as a **brine (20% w/v NaCl solution)** to prevent overworking and gritiness. The brine addition must be calculated to account for the water it introduces into the moisture phase of butter. Always verify the final moisture content stays within the **FSSAI 16% max** limit.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Butter Variants vs. Process Parameters

| Variant | Cream Fat (%) | Churning Temp (°C) | Fermentation | Salt (%) | Target pH | Aroma Profile |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Sweet Cream Butter | 35–40 | 7–12 | None | 0–2 | 6.5–6.8 | Mild, fresh |
| Cultured (European) | 38–42 | 12–16 | 16–18 h, 18–22 °C | 0–1.5 | 4.6–4.8 | Tangy, diacetyl-rich |
| Salted (American) | 40 | 8–11 | None | 1.5–2.0 | 6.4–6.7 | Salty, mild |
| Whey Butter | 25–30 (recombined) | 10–14 | Optional | 0–1.5 | 5.5–6.5 | Mild, slightly sweet |
| Lactic Butter | 38–42 | 12–15 | 24 h, 20 °C | 0–1 | 4.4–4.6 | Pronounced acidic |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Batch Butter Manufacturing Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Raw Milk Reception] --> C[Centrifugal Cream Separation]
    C --> S[Standardization to 38% fat]
    S --> P[HTST Pasteurization 72C 15s]
    P --> V[Vacuum De-aeration]
    V --> A1[Aging 4 to 6 C for 12 to 24 h]
    A1 --> D{Is Cultured Butter?}
    D -- Yes --> I[Inoculation with Lactic Starter]
    I --> F[Fermentation 18 to 22 C for 16 h]
    F --> CH
    D -- No --> CH[Churning at 8 to 12 C]
    CH --> BM[Buttermilk Drainage]
    BM --> W[Water Washing twice]
    W --> SA[Salting 2% NaCl]
    SA --> WK[Working and Kneading]
    WK --> T[Tempering at 15 to 20 C]
    T --> PK[Packaging Foil Laminated]
    PK --> ST[Storage 4 C]
    ST --> M[Market Distribution]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Continuous Butter Maker (CBM) Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph F1[FEED SECTION]
        CR[Cream Inlet 40% Fat] --> PR[Plate Pasteurizer]
        PR --> AR[Aging Tank 4C]
    end

    subgraph F2[CHURNING SECTION]
        AR --> CHR[High Speed Churning Cylinder]
        CHR --> GT[Grit Removal]
    end

    subgraph F3[SEPARATION SECTION]
        GT --> SP[Separator Section]
        SP --> BMD[Buttermilk Outlet]
    end

    subgraph F4[WORKING SECTION]
        SP --> WK1[First Working Section]
        WK1 --> SL[Salting Injection]
        SL --> WK2[Second Working Section]
    end

    subgraph F5[OUTPUT SECTION]
        WK2 --> MO[Molding and Extrusion]
        MO --> WRP[Wrapping Machine]
        WRP --> QC[Quality Control Check]
        QC --> ST2[Cold Storage 4C]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Butter Emulsion Inversion Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1 Liquid Cream O-W Emulsion] --> B[Stage 2 Churning Initiates]
    B --> C[Stage 3 Fat Globule Membrane Disruption]
    C --> D[Stage 4 Partial Coalescence Begins]
    D --> E[Stage 5 Fat Cluster Network Forms]
    E --> F[Stage 6 Phase Inversion O-W to W-O]
    F --> G[Stage 7 Buttermilk Expulsion]
    G --> H[Stage 8 Working Yields Plastic Butter]
    H --> I[Final Butter W-O Emulsion]

    A -.contains.-> A1[Fat Globules 0.1 to 10 um with MFGM coating]
    A -.contains.-> A2[Continuous Water Phase with SNF]
    I -.contains.-> I1[Continuous Fat Phase with triglycerides]
    I -.contains.-> I2[Dispersed Water Droplets 5 to 20 um]
    I -.contains.-> I3[Air Cells 1 to 5% v-v]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The "O-W" and "W-O" notation represents **O/W (Oil-in-Water)** and **W/O (Water-in-Oil)** emulsion typology, a critical concept in food colloid science.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define butter as per FSSAI standards. List the major compositional components of butter with their typical percentages.**

**Model Answer:**

Butter, as defined by the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011 (FSSAI)**, is a dairy product manufactured exclusively from milk or cream, characterized by:

- **Milk Fat:** Not less than **80% w/w**
- **Moisture:** Not more than **16% w/w**
- **Milk Solids Not Fat (SNF):** Not more than **3% w/w**
- **Salt (salted variants only):** Not more than **3% w/w**

Major compositional components and their typical proportions:

1. **Milk Fat (80–82%):** Primary phase, mixture of triglycerides, source of fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, K, and β-carotene.
2. **Moisture (15.5–17%):** Dispersed phase, contains dissolved lactose, whey proteins, and mineral salts.
3. **SNF (1–2%):** Casein, whey proteins, lactose, and ash.
4. **Salt (0–3%):** Sodium chloride added for flavor, preservation, and shelf-life extension.
5. **Occluded Air (1–5% v/v):** Incorporated during churning; affects density and color.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Explain the role of churning in butter manufacture. Why is aging of cream necessary before churning?**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Churning:**

Churning is the **mechanical agitation step** in butter manufacture that physically transforms cream into butter. Its functions include:

1. **Destabilization of the milk fat globule membrane (MFGM)** through shear and collision of fat globules.
2. **Promotion of partial coalescence**, where fat globules cluster and form a 3D interconnected fat network.
3. **Phase inversion** from an **O/W (fat-in-water) emulsion** to a **W/O (water-in-fat) emulsion** when the fat concentration reaches ~80%.
4. **Expulsion of buttermilk** (the aqueous phase) which is drained off.
5. **Incorporation of air** (1–5% by volume), which gives butter its characteristic light texture and pale yellow color.

**Necessity of Cream Aging:**

Aging (cooling cream to 4–6 °C and holding for 12–24 hours) is essential because:

1. **Crystallization of high-melting triglycerides** produces the optimal **solid fat content (SFC)** required for partial coalescence.
2. **Increase in cream viscosity** enhances the efficiency of fat globule collision during churning.
3. **Equilibration of fat crystals** with the liquid oil phase ensures a stable, plastic texture in the final butter.
4. Without aging, the cream produces butter with poor texture (oily or greasy) and reduced yield.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 - Module 1 Choice]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **manufacturing process of salted table butter** from raw milk, with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the role of pasteurization and aging steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dairy plant receives **5,000 kg of cream** with **38% fat** content. The butter produced contains **82% fat**, and the **fat recovery efficiency is 98.0%**. Calculate: **(i)** Mass of butter produced, **(ii)** Mass and volume of buttermilk (assume density = 1.03 kg/L), **(iii)** Percentage overrun (fat lost in buttermilk). **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution (a):

The **manufacturing process of salted table butter** consists of the following sequential steps:

**Step 1: Reception of Raw Milk**
Raw milk is received, weighed, sampled, and tested for quality (acidity, fat content, microbial load, antibiotic residues). Acceptable milk is clarified/filtered to remove extraneous matter.

**Step 2: Cream Separation**
A **centrifugal cream separator** is used to split whole milk into **skim milk** (~0.05% fat) and **cream** (~35–40% fat). The separator exploits **Stoke's Law** to separate the lighter fat globules from the denser aqueous phase.

**Step 3: Standardization of Cream**
Cream is standardized to a **target fat content (typically 35–40%)** using the **Pearson Square method** by blending with skim milk.

**Step 4: Pasteurization**
Cream is heat-treated using **HTST (72 °C / 15 sec)** or **UHT (135 °C / 4 sec)** pasteurization. Objectives:
- Eliminate pathogens (*Mycobacterium bovis*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, *Salmonella spp.*)
- Inactivate native milk **lipases** (preventing lipolysis and rancidity)
- Standardize viscosity and microbial load

**Step 5: Aging (Cool Maturation)**
Cream is rapidly cooled to **4–6 °C** and held for **12–24 hours** in aging tanks. This step:
- Crystallizes the higher-melting triglycerides (palmitic, stearic fractions) ensuring the right SFC
- Increases viscosity for efficient churning
- Allows complete solidification of fat for plasticity

**Step 6: Churning**
Cream is churned in a **batch churn** or **continuous butter maker** at **8–12 °C** for **30–45 minutes**. The agitation:
- Disrupts the MFGM
- Induces partial coalescence of fat globules
- Inverts the emulsion (O/W → W/O)
- Expels buttermilk (released aqueous phase)

**Step 7: Buttermilk Drainage and Washing**
Buttermilk (0.4–0.7% fat) is drained. The butter granules are **washed twice with cold water (4–5 °C)** to remove residual lactose and buttermilk solids, thereby improving shelf life.

**Step 8: Salting**
**Dry salt (NaCl) at 1.5–2.0%** is sprinkled on the butter, or **20% brine** is injected. Salt enhances:
- Flavor
- Microbial shelf life (water activity reduction)
- Preservation by inhibiting spoilage flora

**Step 9: Working (Kneading)**
The butter is worked using butter-worker rollers to:
- Distribute the moisture as **fine droplets (5–20 µm)**
- Evenly disperse salt
- Achieve plastic, smooth texture

**Step 10: Tempering**
Butter is held at **15–20 °C for 12–48 h** to stabilize the polymorphic form of fat crystals (β'-form), ensuring the **plasticity** of butter at refrigeration temperatures.

**Step 11: Packaging and Storage**
Butter is packaged in **foil-laminate wraps, parchment paper, or plastic tubs** and stored at **4 °C (or -18 °C for long-term storage)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Role of Pasteurization:** Ensures **microbial safety**, inactivates enzymes, and creates a uniform cream quality.
> **Role of Aging:** Promotes **fat crystallization**, viscosity increase, and churn efficiency.

---

### Model Solution (b):

**Given:**
- $M_{cream} = 5000$ kg
- $F_{cream} = 38\%$
- $F_{butter} = 82\%$
- $\eta_{recovery} = 98.0\%$

**(i) Mass of butter produced:**

**Step 1:** Calculate mass of fat in cream.

$$M_{fat, cream} = 5000 \times 0.38 = 1900 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2:** Apply recovery efficiency.

$$M_{fat, butter} = 1900 \times 0.98 = 1862 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate butter mass.

$$M_{butter} = \frac{1862}{0.82} = 2270.73 \text{ kg}$$

**(ii) Mass and volume of buttermilk:**

**Step 4:** Mass of buttermilk (mass balance).

$$M_{buttermilk} = 5000 - 2270.73 = 2729.27 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5:** Volume of buttermilk.

$$V_{buttermilk} = \frac{2729.27}{1.03} = 2649.78 \text{ L}$$

**(iii) Percentage fat lost (overrun calculation):**

**Step 6:** Mass of fat in buttermilk.

$$M_{fat, buttermilk} = 1900 - 1862 = 38 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 7:** Percentage fat lost.

$$\% \text{Fat loss} = \frac{38}{1900} \times 100 = 2.0\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answers:**
> **(i)** Butter mass = **2270.73 kg**
> **(ii)** Buttermilk mass = **2729.27 kg**; Volume = **2649.78 L**
> **(iii)** Fat lost = **2.0%** (which is the inverse of 98% recovery efficiency — as expected)

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 - Module 1 Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Compare **batch and continuous butter making processes** in terms of equipment, throughput, energy efficiency, and product quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **cultured butter**? Explain the **fermentation step** in detail, including the role of starter cultures, diacetyl formation, and pH control. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution (a):

**Comparison of Batch vs. Continuous Butter Making Processes**

| Parameter | Batch Process (Fritz Churn) | Continuous Process (Westfalia, GEA) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Capacity** | 500–10,000 L per churn | 5,000–25,000 L/h |
| **Cycle Time** | 30–45 min per batch | < 2 min residence time |
| **Equipment** | Stainless steel rotating churn | High-speed cylindrical churn + separator + working section |
| **Labor** | High (manual loading/unloading) | Low (automated PLC control) |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Lower (intermittent operation) | Higher (continuous, optimized) |
| **Texture Control** | Less precise | Highly precise (crystal control) |
| **Capital Cost** | Lower initial | High initial, fast ROI |
| **Product Uniformity** | Batch-to-batch variation | High batch-to-batch consistency |
| **Microbial Load** | Slightly higher | Lower (closed system) |
| **Flexibility** | Easy to switch product types | Less flexible, optimized for single product |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Continuous butter makers dominate the **modern industrial dairy** landscape due to throughput, consistency, and lower per-unit cost. Batch systems are retained for **artisanal, premium, and small-scale operations** (e.g., French *Beurre d'Isigny*, Irish *Kerrygold*).

---

### Model Solution (b):

**Cultured Butter — Definition and Fermentation Process**

**Definition:** Cultured butter (also called **lactic butter** or **European-style butter**) is butter produced from **cream that has been fermented by lactic acid bacteria** before churning. It is characterized by a **tangy flavor**, **softer texture**, and **enhanced aroma** due to fermentation by-products.

**Fermentation Process:**

**Step 1: Inoculation**
After pasteurization, cream is cooled to **18–22 °C** and inoculated with a **mesophilic starter culture** consisting of:
- *Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis* (acid producer)
- *Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *cremoris* (acid producer, smoother texture)
- *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* subsp. *cremoris* (aroma/diacetyl producer)
- Sometimes *Citrate-positive Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis* biovar *diacetylactis*

**Step 2: Fermentation Incubation**
The inoculated cream is held at **18–22 °C for 16–18 hours** (or until titratable acidity reaches **0.7–0.9% lactic acid**, and pH drops to **4.6–4.8**).

**Step 3: Biochemical Conversions**
During fermentation:
- **Lactose** is converted to **lactic acid** (homolactic fermentation): $C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} + H_2O \rightarrow 4 \times C_3H_6O_3$
- **Citric acid** is metabolized by *Leuconostoc* to **diacetyl** (2,3-butanedione) — the principal **butter aroma compound**
- **Acetaldehyde** and other volatile acids contribute to flavor complexity

**Step 4: Cooling and Churning**
Fermented cream is cooled to churning temperature (12–15 °C) and churned. The **low pH** modifies the milk protein interactions, producing butter with a **softer, more spreadable texture** and **distinctive tangy taste**.

**Role of Diacetyl:**
Diacetyl is the **key aroma compound** responsible for the characteristic **buttery, nutty, slightly sweet** aroma. Its concentration in cultured butter is **10–50× higher** than in sweet cream butter.

**pH Control:**
- pH **4.6–4.8** is the isoelectric point of casein → milk proteins are **least soluble** → easy separation during churning
- Acidic pH acts as a **natural preservative** (inhibits spoilage bacteria)
- pH below 4.5 results in **over-acidification** (harsh, undesirable flavor)

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Insight:** Cultured butter commands a **20–40% price premium** in markets (especially in Europe and Japan) compared to sweet cream butter. Major brands include *President*, *Lurpak*, *Isigny Sainte-Mère*, and *Kerrygold*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Failing to state the FSSAI compositional standards** (80% fat, 16% moisture, 3% SNF) in definition questions → **-2 marks**
> 2. **Confusing phase inversion direction** (cream is O/W; butter is W/O; NOT the reverse) → **-2 marks**
> 3. **Skipping the role of MFGM disruption** in the churning process → **-1.5 marks**
> 4. **Numerical errors in fat balance** (forgetting to multiply recovery efficiency) → **-3 marks**
> 5. **Not mentioning aging temperature & time** in process descriptions → **-1.5 marks**
> 6. **Conflating "cultured" with "salted" butter** (these are different process variants) → **-2 marks**
> 7. **Forgetting to convert kg to L using density** in buttermilk volume questions → **-2 marks**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary for Last-Minute Revision**

- **Definition:** Butter = W/O emulsion, ≥80% fat (FSSAI), ≤16% moisture, ≤3% SNF.
- **Emulsion Type:** Fat-continuous (W/O); inverts from cream's O/W during churning.
- **Key Raw Material:** Cream standardized to **35–40% fat**.
- **MFGM:** The milk fat globule membrane is the **natural emulsifier** that must be disrupted for butter formation.
- **Pasteurization:** HTST (**72 °C / 15 sec**) — destroys pathogens and lipases.
- **Aging:** Cream at **4–6 °C for 12–24 h** — promotes fat crystallization and viscosity.
- **Churning Temperature:** **8–12 °C** (sweet cream); **12–16 °C** (cultured).
- **Churning Time:** **30–45 min** (batch); **<2 min** (continuous).
- **Phase Inversion Point:** ~**80% fat concentration**.
- **Washing:** Removes residual lactose → prevents fermentation, extends shelf life.
- **Salt Function:** Flavor + water activity reduction + microbial inhibition (1.5–2% typical).
- **Working:** Distributes moisture as **5–20 µm droplets** → smooth, plastic texture.
- **Tempering:** 15–20 °C for 12–48 h → stabilizes β' polymorph → plasticity at refrigeration.
- **Cultured Butter:** Uses *Lactococcus* and *Leuconostoc* → lactic acid + diacetyl → tangy flavor.
- **Diacetyl (2,3-butanedione):** The **signature aroma compound** of butter.
- **β-Carotene:** Natural yellow pigment from cow feed; higher in grass-fed cows.
- **Yield Equation:** $M_{butter} = \frac{M_{cream} \times F_{cream} \times \eta_{recovery}}{F_{butter}}$
- **Buttermilk Output:** 50–55% of input cream mass, with 0.4–0.7% residual fat.
- **Continuous Butter Maker Brands:** Westfalia, GEA, Tetra Pak, Alfa Laval.
- **Storage Temperature:** 4 °C (short term); -18 °C (long term); 9–12 months shelf life at 4 °C.
- **Common Defects:** Greasy texture (inadequate aging), bland flavor (low diacetyl), mold growth (high moisture >16%), discoloration (oxidized fat).
- **FSSAI Salted Butter Standards:** Fat ≥80%, Moisture ≤16%, SNF ≤3%, Salt ≤3%.
- **Pearson Square Method:** Used for cream standardization calculations.
- **Beurre d'Isigny AOC:** Premium French cultured butter with protected designation of origin.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fermented Meat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT412 - FOOD PROCESS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fermented Meat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fermented Meat** refers to meat products that are preserved and developed in their characteristic sensory profile through the controlled **lactic acid fermentation** of carbohydrates (typically glucose, sucrose, or maltodextrin) added to the meat batter, leading to a rapid decrease in **pH (target final pH 4.5 – 5.3)** and a parallel reduction in **water activity (a<sub>w</sub>)** during subsequent drying/ripening. The combination of low pH, low a<sub>w</sub>, curing salts, and competitive lactic acid bacteria (LAB) flora results in a shelf-stable, microbiologically safe, and flavour-mature product such as *salami, pepperoni, chorizo, summer sausage, and sucuk*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Fermented Products):**
> Fermentation is *not* cooking. It is a **biochemical** preservation technique where **microbes (not heat)** are the primary agents of safety and stability. Meat itself contains very little fermentable sugar; therefore, **exogenous carbohydrates (0.5 – 1.5 % w/w)** must be back-added to drive the pH drop.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Meat Pickle"

Think of fermented meat as the **"pickled cousin of fresh sausage"**:

- When you pickle cucumbers in vinegar, the **acetic acid** (pre-formed) does the preserving.
- In fermented meat, you add sugar to a ground meat batter, stuff it into a casing, and let **friendly bacteria (Lactobacillus, Pediococcus)** *manufacture the acid in situ* from that sugar.
- The acid (mainly **lactic acid**) drops the pH just like vinegar does in a pickle — turning a perishable raw meat into a stable, tangy, dry, sliceable product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway for viva:** Fermentation is essentially a **"self-acidification"** technology. The acid is produced *inside* the product by inoculated or naturally present LAB, not added from outside.

## 1.3 Standard Process Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

| Parameter | Typical Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Final pH** | **4.5 – 5.3** | Microbial safety (inhibits pathogens) |
| **Water activity (a<sub>w</sub>)** | **0.85 – 0.91** | Stops spoilage moulds/yeasts |
| **Moisture : Protein ratio (M:P)** | **≤ 3.0 : 1** | US-FDA compliance for shelf stability |
| **Salt (NaCl) in batter** | **2.5 – 4.0 %** | Selects LAB over pathogens |
| **NaNO<sub>2</sub>** | **100 – 200 ppm** | Clostridium botulinum control |
| **Fermentation temperature** | **20 – 25 °C** | Optimal LAB growth |
| **Fermentation time** | **48 – 72 h** | Until pH ≤ 5.3 is reached |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Fermented Meat — Four Pillars of Stability

A fermented meat product achieves its long shelf life by **simultaneously attacking four hurdles** (a classic application of the *Hurdle Technology* of Leistner, 1992):

1. **pH hurdle (low pH)** – Lactic acid dissociates → H⁺ ions denature microbial enzymes and disrupt membrane transport.
2. **a<sub>w</sub> hurdle (low water activity)** – Achieved by drying/ripening; halts microbial metabolism.
3. **Salt hurdle (high NaCl)** – Lowers a<sub>w</sub> and is selectively toxic to Gram-negative pathogens.
4. **Curing salt hurdle (NaNO<sub>2</sub> / NaNO<sub>3</sub>)** – Forms **nitrosomyoglobin** (red cured colour) and inhibits *Clostridium botulinum* via nitric oxide (NO) action.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** When asked "Why is fermented meat safe without cooking?" — answer with these *four hurdles working together*, not just "because pH is low."

## 2.2 Microbiology of Fermented Meats

| Group | Examples | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB)** | *Lactobacillus plantarum, L. sakei, L. curvatus, Pediococcus pentosaceus, P. acidilactici, Staphylococcus carnosus, S. xylosus* | Primary acidifiers, flavour (diacetyl, acetoin), colour stability |
| **Catalase-positive cocci (non-pathogenic staphylococci & micrococci)** | *Staphylococcus carnosus, S. xylosus* | Reduce NO<sub>2</sub> → NO (colour), produce fruity flavour esters |
| **Yeasts & Moulds (surface ripening)** | *Penicillium nalgiovense, P. candidum, Debaryomyces hansenii* | Surface ripening, oxygen barrier, characteristic flavour |
| **Pathogens (must be controlled)** | *Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, E. coli O157:H7, Clostridium botulinum* | Inhibited by low pH, salt, NO<sub>2</sub> |

## 2.3 Operational Flow — Six Logical Steps

1. **Raw material selection** – Lean meat + back-fat (typical 70:30 to 80:20) chilled to **< 4 °C**.
2. **Comminution (grinding/milling)** – Reduces particle size, distributes salt and starter uniformly. Two-stage grinding: coarse (8 mm) then fine (3 mm).
3. **Mixing with ingredients** – Salt (2.5–4 %), curing salts (NaNO<sub>2</sub> 100–200 ppm), sugars (glucose/dextrose 0.5–1 %), spices (pepper, garlic, paprika), starter culture (~10⁶–10⁷ CFU/g).
4. **Stuffing** – Into natural or collagen casings of 40 – 100 mm diameter; density should be tight to avoid air pockets.
5. **Fermentation** – Held at **20 – 25 °C, 90 – 95 % RH** for 48 – 72 h until **pH ≤ 5.3**. Surface yeasts (optional) may be sprayed pre-fermentation.
